network.overall_strategy: The network converges on lactaldehyde (2-hydroxypropanal) as the central carbonyl precursor for threonine, following literature consensus (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; meteoritic Strecker analyses). Three major branches feed lactaldehyde synthesis: (1) a hydrothermal CO₂ fixation chain producing formaldehyde and formate, which flow to the surface; (2) a UV cyanosulfidic photoredox cascade (glycolonitrile → glycolaldehyde → acetaldehyde → lactonitrile → lactaldehyde); and (3) an HCN oligomerization branch producing aminomalononitrile (AMN) that reacts directly with acetaldehyde. Downstream, Strecker chemistry (lactaldehyde + HCN + NH₃ → threonine aminonitrile) operates in both surface and hydrothermal settings, and hydrolysis completes the route to threonine. Hub molecules HCN, acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, formaldehyde, and threonine aminonitrile interconnect 10 distinct pathways across hydrothermal, surface, and biochemical environments.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO₂ reduction to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent, pH 9-11, H₂ from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is adsorbed on the greigite (Fe₃S₄) surface which acts as a structural analog of biological [Fe₄S₄] clusters. H₂ provides reducing equivalents, and CO₂ is reduced stepwise via a surface-bound formate intermediate. Greigite facilitates two-electron transfer to convert CO₂ to HCOO⁻.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO₂ reduction to formate is well-established in the iron-sulfur world hypothesis. Andersson & Holm (2000) discuss hydrothermal reduction of CO₂ under mineral-buffered redox conditions with pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite assemblages. This step supplies the C1 feedstock for downstream formaldehyde synthesis.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reduction of formate to formaldehyde on magnetite

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch type reduction on magnetite surface, subseafloor hydrothermal

reactions.mechanism: Formate is further reduced on magnetite surfaces via Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry. Fe⁰ or Fe²⁺ in magnetite provides additional reducing power. Formate is reduced to formaldehyde through loss of one oxygen as H₂O, catalyzed by mineral surface adsorption and electron transfer.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis on magnetite producing aldehydes including formaldehyde is documented in the hydrothermal prebiotic chemistry literature. Andersson & Holm (2000) describe mineral-buffered redox conditions supporting aldehyde formation. Formaldehyde is the essential C1 building block for downstream cyanohydrin (glycolonitrile) synthesis. Ritson & Sutherland (2013) note that formaldehyde feeds into the cyanosulfidic network.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation: formaldehyde + HCN → glycolonitrile

conditions.other: Mildly alkaline evaporitic pool, pH 7-9, cyanide concentrated via ferrocyanide thermal processing

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of CN⁻ to the carbonyl of formaldehyde yields glycolonitrile (2-hydroxyacetonitrile) through standard cyanohydrin formation. Clay surfaces concentrate both reactants via adsorption.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (2013, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.) use glycolonitrile as the explicit starting substrate for their UV photoredox cascade. They note that cyanide can be concentrated by complexation with Fe²⁺ to form ferrocyanide, and thermal processing of ferrocyanide salts yields cyanide, ammonia, and cyanamide upon rehydration—reagents directly relevant to Strecker chemistry. Glycolonitrile is the standard cyanohydrin of formaldehyde and a well-known prebiotic molecule.

reactions.name: UV cyanosulfidic photoredox reduction of glycolonitrile to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation at 254 nm, quartz cuvette conditions, 10 mM glycolonitrile, 30 mM H₂S, 33 mM Na₃PO₄, 10 mol% CuCN, 2 h irradiation

reactions.mechanism: CuCN solid generates a fine dark precipitate upon mixing. UV irradiation produces hydrated electrons (e⁻ₐq) via Cu(I)/Cu(II) cyanocuprate photoredox cycling. These hydrated electrons reduce the nitrile group of glycolonitrile to an imine which hydrolyzes to yield glycolaldehyde. H₂S serves as the terminal reductant, regenerating Cu(I).
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (2013, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.) report 42% yield of glycolaldehyde from glycolonitrile under these exact conditions (10 mM glycolonitrile, 30 mM H₂S, 33 mM sodium phosphate, solid CuCN at 10 mol%, 254 nm UV, 2 h). CuCN as heterogeneous photoredox catalyst is explicitly described. Green, Xu & Sutherland (2021, JACS) further discuss Cu(I)/Cu(II) cyanocuprate photoredox cycling as the mechanistic basis. Ritson & Sutherland note dissolution of copper sulfide minerals in cyanide solution as a plausible geochemical source of CuCN.

reactions.name: UV photochemical conversion of glycolaldehyde to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation at 254 nm, photochemical deoxygenation/rearrangement; detected alongside glycolaldehyde in Ritson–Sutherland product mixture

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven photochemical reduction or rearrangement of glycolaldehyde produces acetaldehyde via loss of the hydroxyl group through radical or hydrated-electron-mediated deoxygenation. Acetaldehyde is identified as a co-product in the glycolonitrile photoredox reaction mixture.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (2013, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.) explicitly report acetaldehyde as a product detected alongside glycolaldehyde in the UV photoredox product mixture. Green, Xu & Sutherland (2021, JACS) describe the broader network in which glycolaldehyde-level products undergo further photoreduction. Acetaldehyde is the critical branch point: its cyanohydrin (lactonitrile) is the direct precursor to lactaldehyde.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation: acetaldehyde + HCN → lactonitrile

conditions.other: Mildly alkaline pool, pH 7-9, CN⁻ nucleophilic addition to acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of CN⁻ to the carbonyl carbon of acetaldehyde produces lactonitrile (acetaldehyde cyanohydrin, 2-hydroxypropanenitrile). The reaction is spontaneous in alkaline aqueous solution. Clay mineral surfaces serve to concentrate both HCN and acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (2013, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.) explicitly describe acetaldehyde cyanohydrin (lactonitrile) as the substrate for the second-stage photoredox reduction that yields lactaldehyde. This cyanohydrin formation is a standard, well-precedented aqueous reaction. The meteoritic Strecker-cyanohydrin analysis (Kyushu University report) also identifies cyanohydrin intermediates as central to amino acid formation.

reactions.name: UV cyanosulfidic photoredox reduction of lactonitrile to lactaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation at 254 nm, 2 h; acetaldehyde cyanohydrin as substrate, H₂S as terminal reductant; 19% yield of lactaldehyde reported

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation of the CuCN/H₂S system generates hydrated electrons via Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling. These electrons reduce the nitrile group of lactonitrile (acetaldehyde cyanohydrin) to an imine, which undergoes hydrolysis to yield lactaldehyde (2-hydroxypropanal). H₂S serves as terminal electron donor.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (2013, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.) explicitly report: 'irradiation of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin furnished lactaldehyde in 19% yield (after 2 h irradiation). Lactaldehyde is explicitly identified as the Strecker precursor of (allo-)threonine.' This is the critical bottleneck step producing the dedicated threonine carbonyl precursor. Green, Xu & Sutherland (2021, JACS) reiterate this step in their broader systems-chemistry framework.

reactions.name: Surface Strecker synthesis: lactaldehyde + HCN + NH₃ → threonine aminonitrile

conditions.other: Evaporitic pool with concentrated NH₃ and HCN; days to weeks for aminonitrile formation; produces both threonine and allothreonine diastereomeric aminonitriles

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with NH₃ to form an imine (Schiff base) via condensation with loss of water. Nucleophilic addition of CN⁻ (from HCN) to the imine carbon yields threonine aminonitrile (2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile). Both (2R,3S) and (2S,3R) diastereomers form, corresponding to threonine and allothreonine aminonitriles.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (2013, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.) report that 'when HCN and NH₃ were added to photoreduction products, lactaldehyde was smoothly converted via its cyanohydrin to the (allo-)threonine Strecker intermediate aminonitrile (aminonitrile 22 in the paper numbering).' Timescales of days to weeks are noted. The meteoritic Strecker-cyanohydrin discussion (Kyushu University report) explicitly identifies lactaldehyde as the carbonyl precursor for threonine/allothreonine and emphasizes that ammonia-rich conditions favor aminonitrile over cyanohydrin pathways.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: Aqueous hydrolysis, mildly acidic to neutral pH; nitrile → amide → carboxylic acid; produces racemic threonine + allothreonine

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise hydrolysis of the nitrile group: (1) water addition to the C≡N bond yields an amide intermediate (threonine amide), (2) further hydrolysis of the amide yields the free α-amino acid (threonine). Both diastereomers (threonine and allothreonine) are produced in approximately equal amounts.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of Strecker aminonitriles to amino acids is the expected final step in all Strecker frameworks. The meteoritic Strecker-cyanohydrin discussion (Kyushu University report) explicitly frames hydrolysis of aminonitriles as producing α-amino acids. Parker et al. (2011, PNAS) and Ring et al. (1972, PNAS) confirm threonine and allothreonine among final products, both racemic within measurement uncertainty—supporting abiotic Strecker-type origin.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to aminomalononitrile (AMN)

conditions.other: Concentrated HCN solution, pH 8-9, evaporitic or ice-eutectic concentration; HCN dimer → trimer (AMN); UV may accelerate initial oligomerization

reactions.mechanism: Sequential oligomerization of HCN: (1) 2 HCN → iminoacetonitrile (HCN dimer), (2) addition of third HCN → aminomalononitrile (AMN, HCN trimer). AMN is a key branch point in HCN chemistry, also leading toward adenine (5 HCN) and other heterocycles. Clay surfaces help concentrate HCN above the critical threshold for oligomerization.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization to AMN is well-established in prebiotic chemistry. Thanassi (1975, J. Mol. Evol.) uses AMN as an explicit starting material for amino acid synthesis including threonine/allothreonine. Green, Xu & Sutherland (2021, JACS) discuss the broader HCN chemistry challenge space including AMN accumulation vs. competing polymerization. AMN prebiotic plausibility hinges on whether it can accumulate at useful concentrations.

reactions.name: AMN + acetaldehyde electrophilic coupling → threonine aminonitrile precursor → threonine aminonitrile

conditions.other: Mild aqueous conditions; AMN acts as multifunctional C-N synthon; aldol-type addition of AMN to acetaldehyde followed by partial hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: AMN acts as a nucleophilic carbon-nitrogen synthon, reacting with acetaldehyde as the electrophilic partner. The α-carbon of AMN attacks the carbonyl of acetaldehyde, generating a C4 adduct bearing amino, cyano, and hydroxyl functionalities. Partial hydrolysis and rearrangement yields the threonine aminonitrile equivalent. Full hydrolysis then yields threonine/allothreonine.
reactions.reasoning: Thanassi (1975, J. Mol. Evol.) provides direct experimental evidence that aminomalononitrile-derived chemistry yields threonine and allothreonine after hydrolysis when AMN is reacted with electrophiles (including aldehydes) under mild conditions. This route bypasses the need to pre-form lactaldehyde in bulk, offering an alternative C-N assembly pathway. The tradeoff is that selectivity, geochemical plausibility of AMN accumulation, and competing polymerization remain active questions (Green et al., 2021, JACS).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Strecker synthesis: lactaldehyde + HCN + NH₃ → threonine aminonitrile (mineral-buffered)

conditions.other: Redox-buffered by pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite assemblage; pH controlled by K-feldspar-muscovite-quartz; requires prior delivery of lactaldehyde from surface photochemistry or hydrothermal aldol pathways

reactions.mechanism: Strecker synthesis under hydrothermal conditions: lactaldehyde condenses with NH₃ to form an imine, followed by CN⁻ addition to yield threonine aminonitrile. The mineral buffer assemblage constrains redox potential (oxygen fugacity) to prevent oxidative degradation. Elevated temperature accelerates both imine formation and CN⁻ addition but also risks hydroxy amino acid degradation.
reactions.reasoning: Andersson & Holm (2000, Origins of Life and Evolution of the Biosphere) describe hydrothermal Strecker experiments (Hennet et al., 1992) at 150°C and 10 bar using pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite mineral buffers that yielded glycine, alanine, aspartate, and glutamate. Threonine was not explicitly tabulated, highlighting a practical bottleneck: lactaldehyde availability and hydroxy amino acid thermal degradation. This reaction is included as a plausible extrapolation of demonstrated hydrothermal Strecker chemistry, with the caveat that threonine-specific demonstration remains incomplete. Keefe et al. (1995, PNAS) caution that direct FeS/H₂S-driven CO₂-to-amino-acid routes failed, reinforcing that carbonyls + CN⁻ (Strecker) rather than direct CO₂ fixation is the operative pathway.

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal-to-surface cyanosulfidic photoredox pathway
pathways.description: CO₂ is fixed to formate then formaldehyde in hydrothermal vents. Formaldehyde enters the surface cyanosulfidic photoredox cascade via glycolonitrile → glycolaldehyde → acetaldehyde → lactonitrile → lactaldehyde, followed by surface Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface with hydrothermal Strecker finish
pathways.description: Same upstream chain as P1 (CO₂ → formate → formaldehyde → glycolonitrile → glycolaldehyde → acetaldehyde → lactonitrile → lactaldehyde), but the final Strecker step occurs under hydrothermal mineral-buffered conditions instead of surface conditions.

pathways.name: Direct surface cyanosulfidic photoredox pathway (exogenous formaldehyde)
pathways.description: Formaldehyde delivered by meteoritic/cometary infall or atmospheric photochemistry enters the surface cyanosulfidic cascade directly, bypassing hydrothermal CO₂ fixation.

pathways.name: Acetaldehyde-entry photoredox pathway
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde from volcanic/atmospheric sources or glycolaldehyde photoreduction enters the pathway at the cyanohydrin step, yielding lactonitrile → lactaldehyde → threonine aminonitrile → threonine.

pathways.name: Lactaldehyde-entry surface Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Pre-formed lactaldehyde (e.g., from meteoritic delivery or other photochemical sources) undergoes direct Strecker synthesis on the surface to yield threonine aminonitrile, followed by hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Lactaldehyde-entry hydrothermal Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Pre-formed lactaldehyde undergoes Strecker synthesis under hydrothermal mineral-buffered conditions, followed by hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: AMN + acetaldehyde route via HCN oligomerization
pathways.description: HCN oligomerizes to aminomalononitrile (AMN) on clay surfaces; AMN reacts with acetaldehyde (from glycolaldehyde photoreduction) as an electrophilic partner to yield threonine aminonitrile, followed by hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal-to-AMN route
pathways.description: CO₂ is fixed hydrothermally to formaldehyde, which enters the photoredox cascade to produce acetaldehyde; independently, HCN oligomerizes to AMN; AMN and acetaldehyde combine to yield threonine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: AMN + direct acetaldehyde route
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde from exogenous or atmospheric sources combined with AMN from HCN oligomerization, bypassing the full photoredox cascade. A shorter alternative for environments with pre-existing acetaldehyde.

pathways.name: Surface photoredox to hydrothermal Strecker via acetaldehyde cyanohydrin
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde formed photochemically is converted to lactonitrile then lactaldehyde on the surface; lactaldehyde is then transported to hydrothermal flanks where mineral-buffered Strecker synthesis occurs under higher temperature/pressure, followed by hydrolysis.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the most complete pathway, spanning all three environments. Hydrothermal CO₂ fixation to formate (rxn_001) is well-supported. The formate-to-formaldehyde step (rxn_002) is thermodynamically challenging even on magnetite, and the glycolaldehyde-to-acetaldehyde photochemical step (rxn_005) lacks a clean mechanistic account—literature describes acetaldehyde as a minor side product, not a high-yield direct conversion. The downstream cyanosulfidic photoredox and Strecker steps are strongly literature-supported (Ritson & Sutherland 2013; 42% yield for glycolaldehyde, 19% for lactaldehyde).
suggestions: Clarify mechanism for glycolaldehyde → acetaldehyde photochemical step; consider an aldol/retro-aldol or decarbonylation pathway with explicit intermediates
Consider an alternative formaldehyde source (e.g., photoreduction of CO or meteoritic delivery) to bypass the thermodynamically difficult formate → formaldehyde reduction

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Similar to P1 but finishes with hydrothermal Strecker (rxn_012). Literature explicitly notes threonine was NOT among tabulated hydrothermal Strecker products (Andersson & Holm 2000), and hydroxy amino-acid degradation at high temperatures is a known concern. The mineral-buffered claim is reasonable but underspecified. The surface→hydrothermal transition for lactaldehyde is directionally unusual though not impossible.
suggestions: Specify mineral buffer for rxn_012 (e.g., pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite as per Andersson & Holm)
Address hydroxy amino-acid thermal degradation risk at hydrothermal temperatures

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is essentially the Ritson & Sutherland cyanosulfidic photoredox route and is the most directly literature-supported. By assuming exogenous formaldehyde it avoids the weakest hydrothermal steps but loses the cross-environmental flow. The rxn_005 step remains the weakest link. All Surface→Biochemical transitions are clean.
suggestions: Explicitly note CuCN as photoredox catalyst for surface steps (per Ritson & Sutherland)
Address formaldehyde source (meteoritic, photochemical CO reduction, or Cannizzaro from formose)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A compact and chemically strong pathway. Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin photoreduction to lactaldehyde (19% yield, Ritson & Sutherland) followed by Strecker is well-demonstrated. Environmental progression is limited (surface→biochemical only), reducing network contribution. All steps have strong experimental support.
suggestions: Specify acetaldehyde source to strengthen the entry point

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The minimal Strecker module: lactaldehyde + HCN + NH₃ → threonine aminonitrile → threonine. Extremely well-supported by Strecker literature and meteoritic analogy. Too short to be a standalone network contribution but serves as the convergence module for the whole network.
suggestions: This is really a convergence module, not a standalone pathway; its value is in receiving products from multiple upstream routes

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrothermal Strecker from lactaldehyde. The literature caution is significant: threonine is not explicitly demonstrated in hydrothermal Strecker experiments, and hydroxy amino-acid degradation is a documented concern. The mineral buffering claim needs specification. Hydrothermal→Biochemical transition is acceptable.
suggestions: Specify temperature, pressure, and mineral buffer (e.g., 150°C, pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite)
Address hydroxy amino-acid thermal stability explicitly
Consider lower-temperature flank settings rather than high-T vent core

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The AMN route is supported by Thanassi (1975) but the mechanism for AMN + acetaldehyde → threonine aminonitrile (rxn_011) is underspecified. Thanassi reports threonine/allothreonine in hydrolysates, but the intermediate steps are murky. AMN accumulation at useful concentrations is flagged as an open question in the literature. The rxn_005 weakness compounds uncertainty.
suggestions: Elaborate the mechanism for rxn_011: is this a Knoevenagel-type condensation followed by reduction/hydrolysis?
Address AMN stability and concentration issues
Add a hydrolysis step explicitly if needed between rxn_011 product and threonine aminonitrile

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The longest AMN route inherits all weaknesses of P7 plus the hydrothermal uncertainties. Good environmental flow (hydrothermal→surface→biochemical) but accumulated uncertainty across 8 steps reduces confidence. Multiple weak links (rxn_002, rxn_005, rxn_011) compound.
suggestions: Strengthen weakest links or provide alternative bypasses
Consider whether this long route adds value beyond P1 and P7 individually

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Minimal AMN route. Compact but hinges entirely on rxn_011 (AMN + acetaldehyde), which is the least mechanistically specified reaction in the network. Thanassi support is real but indirect. Environmental progression is limited (surface only + biochemical).
suggestions: Provide detailed mechanism for AMN + acetaldehyde coupling
Specify conditions (pH, temperature, catalysts)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Nicely demonstrates the surface-to-hydrothermal transition through lactonitrile photoreduction and hydrothermal Strecker. The surface photoredox steps are well-supported. The hydrothermal Strecker for threonine specifically remains a caveat but at lower temperature flanks this could work. Good environmental bridging.
suggestions: Specify lower-temperature hydrothermal conditions to minimize degradation
Cite Hennet et al. 1992 for hydrothermal Strecker precedent

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-designed synthesis network for threonine that closely follows the literature consensus. The central strategy of converging on lactaldehyde as the key carbonyl precursor, followed by Strecker chemistry, is strongly supported by Ritson & Sutherland (2013), Green et al. (2021), and meteoritic Strecker analyses. The network exhibits genuine convergence with 5 convergence points and multiple alternative routes to threonine aminonitrile (surface Strecker, hydrothermal Strecker, and AMN coupling). Three main strengths: (1) excellent literature alignment with the cyanosulfidic photoredox route providing quantitative yield data, (2) proper cross-environmental flow from hydrothermal CO₂ fixation through surface photoredox to biochemical hydrolysis, and (3) inclusion of the alternative AMN route (Thanassi 1975) for robustness. Three weaknesses: (1) HCN and NH₃ appear as hub intermediates without production reactions—the network should show their genesis (e.g., volcanic HCN, thermal decomposition of ferrocyanide); (2) rxn_005 (glycolaldehyde → acetaldehyde) lacks a clean mechanistic pathway and is a bottleneck in 6 of 10 pathways; (3) the AMN + acetaldehyde coupling mechanism (rxn_011) needs elaboration. Formaldehyde's designation as a hub is marginal (degree 2). Overall, the network is chemically credible, well-connected, and achieves the target through multiple validated routes.
